metadata.description: TapChill — Complete food and drink database for Indian summer heat survival
metadata.primary_nutrition_source: Indian Food Composition Tables (IFCT) 2017 — National Institute of Nutrition, ICMR, Hyderabad
metadata.ayurvedic_references: Charaka Samhita; Ashtanga Hridayam; Bhavaprakasha Nighantu
metadata.medical_references: NDMA Heatwave Guidelines 2024; WHO Heat-Health Action Plans; Indian Journal of Public Health (heat illness studies)
metadata.image_license_note: All image_url fields use Unsplash Source API (free, attribution not required). Replace with your own CDN-hosted images before production.
metadata.notes: Nutrition values are per 100g of edible portion unless stated otherwise. Electrolyte values from IFCT 2017 where available, USDA otherwise.

foods.name: Watermelon
foods.image_alt: Sliced red watermelon on a plate
foods.why_recommended_in_summer: Watermelon is 91–92% water, making it one of nature's most effective hydrators. The high lycopene content (more than tomatoes) provides powerful antioxidant protection against UV-induced skin damage. Its natural sugars provide quick energy without the metabolic heat burden of complex carbohydrates. In Ayurveda, it is classified as 'sheeta virya' (cooling potency) and is recommended to pacify Pitta dosha during summer. The potassium and magnesium content helps replenish electrolytes lost through sweating. NDMA heatwave guidelines specifically list watermelon among recommended foods during heat emergencies.

steps: Wash the outer rind thoroughly with clean water.
Cut into thick slices or cubes.
Optionally sprinkle with black salt (kala namak) and a squeeze of lemon for enhanced electrolyte benefit.
Serve chilled. For juice: blend cubes without added water, strain if desired, add mint leaves.
preparation.storage_tip: Cut watermelon spoils fast in heat. Refrigerate immediately and consume within 4-6 hours. Never leave cut watermelon at room temperature in summer.
preparation.serving_suggestion: 250g serving (about 2 large slices) provides ~230ml water equivalent

foods.name: Cucumber
foods.why_recommended_in_summer: Cucumber has the highest water content (95–96%) among commonly eaten vegetables, making it the single most hydrating solid food available. In Ayurveda, cucumber is called 'Susheetala' (naturally cool) and is prescribed for excessive thirst, urinary troubles, and heat-related skin irritation. The silica content supports skin health against sun damage. Cucumbers contain fisetin, a flavonoid shown in studies to reduce inflammatory response — important when the body is under heat stress. The extremely low calorie count (15 kcal/100g) means it hydrates without adding metabolic heat load.

steps: Wash thoroughly. Peel if preferred (skin is edible and nutritious).
Slice into rounds or sticks.
Sprinkle with black salt, roasted cumin powder, and lemon juice.
For raita: grate cucumber, mix with whisked curd, add roasted cumin and mint.
preparation.storage_tip: Store whole cucumbers in the refrigerator. Once cut, wrap in cloth and consume within 2-3 hours in summer.

foods.name: Muskmelon (Kharbuja)
foods.image_alt: Ripe muskmelon cut in half showing orange flesh
foods.why_recommended_in_summer: Muskmelon is 90% water and remarkably rich in potassium (267mg/100g) — one of the key electrolytes lost through heavy sweating. It contains nearly 37mg of Vitamin C per 100g, which supports immune function under heat stress. The high Vitamin A content (169 mcg RAE) protects skin from UV damage. In Ayurveda, kharbuja is considered a Pitta-pacifying fruit and is traditionally eaten during peak summer months. The natural sugars provide quick energy without the digestive burden of cooked food.

steps: Wash the outer rind well (bacteria can transfer when cutting).
Cut in half, scoop out seeds.
Slice into wedges or cube the flesh.
Serve chilled. Can be blended into smoothies or milkshakes.
preparation.storage_tip: Whole melon lasts 3-4 days at room temperature. Once cut, refrigerate and consume within 4 hours in summer.
preparation.serving_suggestion: 200g serving provides ~180ml water equivalent plus 534mg potassium

foods.name: Curd / Yogurt (Dahi)
foods.image_alt: Bowl of fresh white curd yogurt
foods.why_recommended_in_summer: Curd is one of India's most powerful summer foods. The Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium probiotics strengthen gut microbiome, which weakens in extreme heat. Curd has a natural cooling effect on the body — unlike milk, fermentation changes its thermal properties from neutral to cooling. The lactic acid aids digestion when appetite drops in heat. Calcium (121mg/100g) supports muscle function affected by dehydration. In Ayurveda, freshly set dahi (not sour/old) is classified as Pitta-pacifying and is the base for buttermilk (chaas), raita, and lassi — all cornerstone summer foods across India.

steps: Best consumed fresh — set curd in the morning for evening use.
Eat plain with a pinch of sugar, or with rice as curd rice.
Add grated cucumber and mint for instant raita.
Whisk with water (1:2 ratio) for quick buttermilk.
Avoid curd at night in Ayurveda — consume before sunset.
preparation.storage_tip: Curd sours rapidly in summer heat. Refrigerate immediately after setting. Consume within 24 hours. Never store in metal containers.
preparation.serving_suggestion: 150g serving with meals. Combine with cucumber for maximum cooling effect.

foods.name: Bottle Gourd (Lauki)
foods.image_alt: Fresh green bottle gourd on wooden surface
foods.why_recommended_in_summer: Bottle gourd (lauki) is 92% water and one of the lightest vegetables to digest, making it ideal when the digestive system slows down in extreme heat. It is one of the most prescribed vegetables in Ayurveda for Pitta dosha imbalance during summer. The very low calorie content (15 kcal/100g) means minimal metabolic heat production during digestion (low thermic effect). Traditional Indian medicine has used lauki juice as a morning coolant for centuries. Combined with rice and buttermilk, it forms what many families across India consider the perfect heat-proof meal.

steps: Peel and cut into small cubes.
IMPORTANT: Taste a tiny piece raw — if bitter, discard the entire gourd (bitter lauki contains toxic tetracyclic triterpenoids).
For sabzi: cook with minimal oil, cumin, turmeric, and salt. Add tomato if desired.
For juice: blend raw lauki with mint and a pinch of salt. Strain and drink fresh.
For dal combo: add lauki cubes while cooking moong dal — makes it lighter.
preparation.storage_tip: Store whole in a cool place. Once cut, refrigerate and use within 24 hours. Cooked lauki spoils very fast — consume immediately.
preparation.serving_suggestion: 200g cooked serving with roti or rice. Best paired with buttermilk.

data_sources.name: IFCT 2017 — NIN, ICMR Hyderabad
data_sources.reference: Entry D009, Bottle gourd (Lagenaria siceraria)

foods.name: Mint (Pudina)
foods.why_recommended_in_summer: Mint contains menthol, which activates TRPM8 cold-sensitive receptors in the mouth and skin, creating a genuine cooling sensation — not just a flavor but a physiological response. This makes mint one of the few foods that provides both perceived and actual cooling. Mint also relaxes smooth muscle in the digestive tract, easing the stomach cramps and nausea common during heat exposure. Its antimicrobial properties help prevent summer food-borne infections. In Ayurveda, pudina is a premier herb for pacifying aggravated Pitta and is added to virtually every summer drink across India.

steps: Wash leaves thoroughly in clean water.
For chutney: grind mint, coriander, green chilli, lemon juice, and salt.
For mint water: muddle 8-10 leaves in a glass, add cold water and lemon.
Add to buttermilk, raita, nimbu pani, jaljeera, or any summer drink.
For iced mint tea: steep leaves in hot water 5 min, cool, add lemon and honey.
preparation.storage_tip: Wrap in damp paper towel and refrigerate. Lasts 4-5 days. Mint wilts fast in heat — never leave at room temperature.
preparation.serving_suggestion: 10-15 leaves per drink or dish. Use liberally — it's very low calorie.

foods.name: Sabja Seeds (Basil Seeds)
foods.image_alt: Soaked sabja basil seeds in a glass of water showing gel coating

nutrition_per_100g.source: USDA FDC #171921 / Published literature on Ocimum basilicum seeds

foods.why_recommended_in_summer: Sabja seeds absorb 30x their weight in water when soaked, forming a gel coat that releases water slowly during digestion — providing sustained hydration unlike plain water which passes through quickly. This gel also creates a feeling of fullness, reducing overeating in summer. The seeds are rich in soluble fiber (22.6g/100g), which aids the sluggish summer digestion. Ayurveda classifies sabja as one of the most potent body coolants, traditionally added to sherbet, falooda, and lemon water across India. They also help control acidity — a common complaint during heatwaves. The iron and calcium content is surprisingly high for a seed consumed in small quantities.

steps: Add 1 teaspoon of sabja seeds to half a glass of water.
Wait 15 minutes until seeds swell and develop a translucent gel coating.
NEVER eat dry/unsoaked sabja seeds — they can swell in the throat and cause choking.
Add soaked seeds to nimbu pani, falooda, milkshakes, sherbet, or kulfi.
Can also be added to smoothie bowls and fruit salads.
preparation.storage_tip: Store dry seeds in an airtight container in a cool, dark place. Once soaked, use within 2-3 hours — do not store soaked seeds.
preparation.serving_suggestion: 1-2 teaspoons per drink (about 5-10g dry). The gel provides the equivalent of an extra 150ml slow-release hydration.

data_sources.name: USDA FoodData Central
data_sources.reference: FDC ID 171921, Basil seed

foods.name: Fennel Seeds (Saunf)
foods.image_alt: Fennel seeds in a wooden bowl
foods.why_recommended_in_summer: Fennel is one of the rare spices classified as 'cooling' in Ayurveda (most spices are heating). It contains anethole, the compound responsible for its sweet aroma, which has documented anti-spasmodic effects on the digestive tract. This makes saunf the go-to after-meal digestive in Indian summer — it settles the stomach without adding heat. The extraordinarily high potassium (1694mg/100g) and calcium (1196mg/100g) values make even a small 5g portion meaningful for electrolyte replenishment. Saunf water (soaked overnight) is a traditional remedy for body heat and acidity across North and West India.

steps: After meals: chew 1/2 teaspoon of saunf directly — the simplest approach.
For saunf water: soak 2 tsp in a glass of water overnight. Strain and drink in the morning.
For fennel tea: steep 1 tsp crushed fennel in hot water 5 min. Cool and drink.
Add to buttermilk, raita, or mint chutney for subtle sweetness.
Popular in paan (betel leaf) — also a cooling after-meal tradition.
preparation.storage_tip: Store in airtight container away from moisture. Lasts 6+ months.
preparation.serving_suggestion: 5g (1 teaspoon) after meals. For saunf water, drink 250ml in the morning.

foods.name: Rice with Curd (Dahi Chawal)
foods.image_alt: Bowl of curd rice garnished with curry leaves and mustard seeds

nutrition_per_100g.source: Calculated from IFCT 2017 entries: cooked rice (A004) + curd (L002), estimated 60:40 ratio

foods.why_recommended_in_summer: Dahi chawal is possibly the most universal Indian summer lunch. Rice is one of the fastest-digesting grains, producing minimal metabolic heat. Combined with cold curd, it creates a probiotic, cooling, easy-to-digest meal that doesn't burden the already-stressed digestive system during heat. The fermentation in curd produces B-vitamins that support energy metabolism. Across South India (thayir sadam), North India (dahi bhat), and Gujarat, curd rice is the default when temperatures cross 40°C. It's also one of the most affordable complete meals — crucial for heat-vulnerable low-income populations.

steps: Cook rice slightly soft (extra water). Cool to room temperature.
Mix with cold fresh curd (not sour) in 1:1 ratio. Add splash of milk if too thick.
Season with salt. Add tempering of mustard seeds, curry leaves, and green chilli in minimal oil.
South Indian style: add grated ginger, chopped coriander, and pomegranate seeds.
North Indian style: mix with sugar instead of salt for sweet version.
Serve at room temperature or slightly cool — do not serve hot.
preparation.storage_tip: Prepare fresh each day. Leftover curd rice ferments further and can cause stomach upset. Never store for more than 3 hours in summer.
preparation.serving_suggestion: 300g serving (1 large bowl) for lunch. Best accompanied by pickle, papad, and buttermilk.

data_sources.name: IFCT 2017 — NIN, ICMR Hyderabad
data_sources.reference: Calculated from A004 (cooked rice) + L002 (curd)

data_sources.name: Traditional knowledge
data_sources.reference: Pan-Indian summer dietary practice documented across regional cuisines

data_sources.name: Journal of Food Science and Technology
data_sources.reference: Glycemic response of curd rice vs plain rice — lower GI when combined with curd

foods.name: Raw Onion
foods.image_alt: Raw red and white onions sliced
foods.why_recommended_in_summer: Raw onion is India's most widespread folk remedy against heatstroke. Outdoor workers, farmers, and construction laborers across India eat raw onion with meals and often carry one in their pocket during peak summer — a practice documented in multiple ethnobotanical studies. Onion contains quercetin, a flavonoid with anti-inflammatory properties that may help the body's heat stress response. The high water content (89%) and natural sulfur compounds are believed to help the body regulate temperature. Note: raw onion is cooling, but cooked onion loses this property. The Indian government's NDMA heatwave advisory mentions consuming raw onion as a traditional preventive measure.

steps: Slice raw onion into rings or thin half-moons.
Serve as side dish with lunch and dinner during peak summer.
Sprinkle with lemon juice and salt — aids absorption.
For onion-mint chutney: grind raw onion with mint, green chilli, and lemon.
IMPORTANT: Must be eaten raw to retain cooling properties. Cooking changes thermal nature.
preparation.storage_tip: Whole onions last weeks in a cool, dry place. Once cut, use within 4-6 hours in summer. Cut onion exposed to heat breeds bacteria rapidly.
preparation.serving_suggestion: 50g (about 3-4 slices) with meals. More if working outdoors.

foods.name: Ash Gourd
foods.image_alt: Ash gourd (petha) cut in half showing white flesh
foods.why_recommended_in_summer: Ash gourd is 96% water — one of the highest water content vegetables. In Ayurveda, it is a premier cooling food classified as 'sheeta virya'. Its juice is traditionally consumed on an empty stomach to reduce body heat. Very low calorie (12 kcal/100g) and high potassium makes it ideal for summer hydration without weight gain.

steps: Peel and remove seeds from fresh ash gourd.
For juice: blend raw pieces with water, strain, add lime and salt.
For sabzi: cook with minimal spices — cumin, turmeric, curry leaves.
Can also be used to make traditional petha (sweet).
preparation.storage_tip: Cut pieces spoil fast in heat. Refrigerate and use within 2 days.
preparation.serving_suggestion: 200ml juice or 150g cooked sabzi as one serving

foods.name: Yogurt / Curd
foods.image_alt: Bowl of fresh white yogurt curd
foods.why_recommended_in_summer: Curd is a natural probiotic that supports gut health, which often weakens in summer. It is 85% water and provides calcium and potassium. Ayurveda classifies it as cooling when consumed before sunset. The lactic acid bacteria help prevent food-borne infections common in summer. Foundation of chaas, lassi, and raita — India's essential summer foods.

steps: Serve fresh, chilled plain curd in a bowl.
For raita: add cucumber, onion, mint, roasted cumin powder.
For curd rice: mix with steamed rice, temper with mustard and curry leaves.
Best consumed at lunch — Ayurveda advises against curd after sunset.
preparation.storage_tip: Always refrigerate. Sour/overfermented curd increases body heat — consume fresh.

foods.name: Raw Onion
foods.image_alt: Sliced red onion rings on a plate
foods.why_recommended_in_summer: Raw onion is a traditional Indian summer food eaten with meals across the country. The quercetin compound offers anti-histamine and anti-inflammatory protection against heat stress. Onion paste applied on the forehead is a folk remedy for heatstroke. NDMA guidelines mention onion among summer-friendly foods. Common practice among outdoor workers, farmers, and labourers during peak heat.

steps: Slice red onion into rings.
Serve as a side with lunch — with lime juice and salt.
Can be added to salad with cucumber and tomato.
Onion-mint chutney is another excellent summer preparation.
preparation.storage_tip: Whole onions last weeks. Cut onions should be used immediately in summer — they attract bacteria fast.

foods.name: Bael Fruit
foods.image_alt: Bael wood apple fruit cut open showing pulp
foods.why_recommended_in_summer: Bael (wood apple) is one of Ayurveda's most prized summer fruits. It treats heat-induced constipation and acts as a natural blood detoxifier. Bael sharbat is recommended to prevent and cure heatstroke. Very high potassium (600mg/100g) replenishes electrolytes rapidly. The India Perspectives government portal specifically recommends bael for preventing adverse effects of dehydration.

steps: Break open the hard shell of ripe bael fruit.
Scoop out the pulp, removing seeds.
Mix pulp with cold water, jaggery or sugar, and a pinch of black pepper.
Strain and serve chilled as bael sharbat.
preparation.storage_tip: Ripe bael spoils fast. Prepare sharbat fresh and consume within hours.
preparation.serving_suggestion: 250ml bael sharbat as a midday cooler

data_sources.name: IFCT 2017 — NIN, ICMR Hyderabad

foods.name: Mint Leaves
foods.image_alt: Fresh green mint pudina leaves bunch
foods.why_recommended_in_summer: Mint contains menthol which triggers cold-sensitive receptors in the body, creating a natural cooling sensation. It aids digestion (which weakens in summer), prevents nausea, and freshens breath. Used in pudina chutney, jaljeera, nimbu pani, and raita — it's the backbone of Indian summer cuisine. Very high iron content (11.9mg/100g) helps counter summer fatigue.

steps: Wash leaves thoroughly in clean water.
Add to nimbu pani, jaljeera, buttermilk, or raita.
For chutney: blend with green chili, coriander, salt, lemon juice.
Garnish on curd rice, salads, or any summer dish.
preparation.storage_tip: Wrap in damp paper towel and refrigerate. Lasts 5-7 days.
preparation.serving_suggestion: 10-15 leaves per serving in any drink or dish

data_sources.name: USDA FoodData Central
data_sources.reference: FDC ID 173474 — Spearmint, fresh

data_sources.name: ICMR
data_sources.reference: Digestive aid recommendation for summer

foods.name: Tender Mango (Kairi)
foods.image_alt: Green raw unripe mangoes on a surface
foods.why_recommended_in_summer: Raw mango (kairi) is the base for aam panna — one of NDMA's recommended drinks for preventing heatstroke. Very high vitamin C (36mg/100g) boosts immunity. The sour taste stimulates saliva production which aids cooling. Traditionally used across India specifically to combat heat-related illness. Different from ripe mango which is heating.

steps: Boil 2-3 raw mangoes until soft (10 min).
Cool, peel, extract pulp.
Blend with water, jaggery, roasted cumin, black salt, mint.
Chill and serve as aam panna.
preparation.storage_tip: Aam panna concentrate can be refrigerated for 3-4 days.

data_sources.name: IFCT 2017 — NIN, ICMR Hyderabad
data_sources.reference: Mango, raw (Mangifera indica)

foods.name: Sugarcane
foods.image_alt: Fresh sugarcane stalks and juice glass
foods.why_recommended_in_summer: Sugarcane juice is a natural energy drink with very high potassium (320mg/100g) — essential for preventing dehydration and heatstroke. It provides instant energy from natural sugars without the crash of processed drinks. Available on virtually every street corner in India during summer. The juice with lime and ginger is a classic Indian summer cooler.

steps: Buy fresh sugarcane juice from a clean vendor.
Ask for lime and ginger to be added (enhances cooling + digestion).
Consume immediately — sugarcane juice oxidizes within 15-20 minutes.
Do not store or refrigerate for later.
preparation.storage_tip: NEVER store sugarcane juice — drink within 15 minutes of extraction. It ferments rapidly.
preparation.serving_suggestion: 250ml fresh juice with lime and ginger

data_sources.name: IFCT 2017 — NIN, ICMR Hyderabad
data_sources.reference: Sugarcane juice (Saccharum officinarum)

data_sources.name: Traditional practice
data_sources.reference: Widely consumed street beverage across India in summer

drinks.name: Nimbu Pani (Indian Lemonade)
drinks.image_alt: Glass of fresh nimbu pani with lemon slices and mint

nutrition_per_serving_250ml.source: Calculated from IFCT 2017 (E037 lemon) + standard recipe proportions

drinks.why_recommended_in_summer: Nimbu pani is India's most accessible and cost-effective rehydration drink. Lemon juice provides citric acid, which enhances the absorption of electrolytes (sodium, potassium) in the gut — this is the same principle behind ORS. The Vitamin C content (18mg per glass) supports immune function weakened by heat stress. Black salt (kala namak) adds trace minerals and the sulfurous compounds aid digestion. The addition of roasted cumin adds carminative properties that settle the stomach. The WHO recognizes homemade sugar-salt solutions (which nimbu pani essentially is) as effective for mild dehydration. At roughly ₹2-3 per glass, it's the most democratic health intervention against heatwaves.

properties.active_compounds: Citric acid (electrolyte absorption), Ascorbic acid (Vitamin C)

ingredients.notes: Fresh only — bottled loses Vitamin C
Cold but not ice-cold (Ayurveda advises against ice)
Jaggery adds iron; sugar dissolves faster
Contains trace minerals absent in table salt

steps: Squeeze the lemon into a glass of cold water.
Add sugar or jaggery and stir until dissolved.
Add black salt and roasted cumin powder.
Crush mint leaves slightly and add to the glass.
Stir well and serve immediately.
preparation.storage_tip: Make fresh each time — do not store. Vitamin C degrades rapidly once lemon is squeezed. Pre-mixing with sugar causes fermentation in heat.

variations.Shikanji (North India): Add a pinch of chaat masala and ginger juice
variations.Jaljeera style: Replace sugar with tamarind paste, add more cumin

data_sources.name: IFCT 2017 — NIN, ICMR Hyderabad
data_sources.reference: Entry E037, Lemon (Citrus limon)

data_sources.name: WHO/UNICEF ORS Guidelines
data_sources.reference: Sugar-salt solution for oral rehydration — same principle as nimbu pani

data_sources.name: NDMA Heatwave Guidelines 2024
data_sources.reference: Listed as recommended drink during heatwave

data_sources.name: Charaka Samhita
data_sources.reference: Nimbuka (lemon) in summer regimen — Ritucharya Adhyaya

drinks.name: Buttermilk (Chaas / Mattha)
drinks.image_alt: Glass of white buttermilk garnished with mint and cumin

nutrition_per_serving_250ml.source: Calculated from IFCT 2017 (L002 curd) diluted 1:2 with water + spices

drinks.why_recommended_in_summer: Chaas is arguably India's most scientifically sound traditional summer drink. The Lactobacillus strains in fermented curd produce lactic acid, which creates an acidic environment that inhibits pathogenic bacteria — critical when foodborne illnesses spike in summer. Buttermilk has a lower caloric density than milk, making it lighter to digest. The added cumin (jeera) is a carminative that prevents the gas and bloating common in heat. Salt replenishes sodium lost through sweat. The calcium (65mg per glass) supports muscle contractions, preventing the cramps that occur during dehydration. In Ayurveda, takra (buttermilk) is called 'amritam' (nectar) and Charaka states that it is the best drink in Grishma Ritu (summer season).

properties.active_compounds: Lactic acid (probiotic), Calcium, B-vitamins from fermentation

ingredients.notes: Finely chopped; skip in extreme heat

steps: Whisk curd until completely smooth with no lumps.
Add cold water gradually while whisking.
Add roasted cumin powder, black salt, and mix well.
Crush mint leaves slightly and add along with chopped coriander.
Stir and serve immediately. Can add 2-3 ice cubes if desired.
preparation.storage_tip: Best consumed fresh. If made in bulk, refrigerate and consume within 4-6 hours. Chaas ferments rapidly in summer heat and can turn sour.

variations.Masala Chaas (Gujarat): Add hing (asafoetida), curry leaves tempering, and extra ginger
variations.Neer Mor (Tamil Nadu): Add ginger, green chilli, curry leaves, asafoetida, and mustard seeds tempering
variations.Majjiga (Andhra Pradesh): Temper with mustard seeds, dried red chilli, and curry leaves in oil
variations.Neer Majige (Karnataka): Simple — curd, water, salt, and a touch of coconut
variations.Rajasthani Chaas: Heavy on cumin and coriander, with hing and mint

data_sources.name: IFCT 2017 — NIN, ICMR Hyderabad
data_sources.reference: Derived from Entry L002 (Curd, cow milk) with dilution factor

drinks.name: Coconut Water
drinks.regional_origin: Coastal India (Kerala, Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Goa, Maharashtra, Odisha, West Bengal, Andhra Pradesh)
drinks.why_recommended_in_summer: Coconut water is nature's isotonic drink — its electrolyte profile (especially potassium at 600mg/250ml) closely matches human plasma, making it more effective for rehydration than plain water. A 2012 study in the Journal of the International Society of Sports Nutrition found coconut water rehydrated as effectively as commercial sports drinks after exercise-induced dehydration, with less stomach upset. The natural sugars (glucose, fructose, sucrose) provide immediate energy without the crash of processed sugars. In Ayurveda, tender coconut (daab) is classified as 'hridya' (beneficial for the heart) and 'trishna-nashaka' (thirst-destroying). It is sterile inside the coconut, making it safe even in areas with contaminated water supply — a critical advantage in rural India during heatwaves.

properties.active_compounds: Natural electrolytes (K, Na, Mg), Cytokinins (plant hormones with anti-aging properties)

ingredients.notes: Choose green tender coconuts (daab), not brown mature ones

steps: Buy fresh tender coconut from a street vendor or store.
Vendor will cut the top open — drink directly with a straw.
After drinking the water, ask vendor to scrape the soft malai (flesh) — also cooling and nutritious.
If buying packed coconut water: check for no added sugar and preservatives.
preparation.storage_tip: Drink immediately once opened. Coconut water oxidizes and loses nutrients within 30 minutes of exposure to air. Packaged coconut water is a compromise — fresh is always superior.

variations.Coconut + sabja: Add soaked sabja seeds for extra cooling
variations.Coconut + lemon: Squeeze half a lemon for Vitamin C boost
variations.Nariyal pani + mint: Muddle mint leaves into coconut water

data_sources.name: USDA FoodData Central
data_sources.reference: FDC ID 170174, Coconut water

data_sources.name: Journal of the International Society of Sports Nutrition
data_sources.reference: Kalman DS et al. — Comparison of coconut water and sports drink on rehydration (2012)

data_sources.name: Molecules (MDPI)

data_sources.name: Bhavaprakasha Nighantu

data_sources.reference: Coconut water recommended as primary hydration source during heatwave

drinks.name: Aam Panna (Raw Mango Drink)
drinks.image_alt: Glass of green aam panna garnished with mint

nutrition_per_serving_250ml.source: Calculated from IFCT 2017 (E042 raw mango) + recipe proportions

drinks.why_recommended_in_summer: Aam panna is one of very few traditional drinks with documented heatstroke-prevention claims supported by both Ayurvedic tradition and basic nutritional science. Raw (unripe) mango is exceptionally rich in Vitamin C — up to 45mg per glass — which is critical for immune function during heat stress. The combination of sugar, salt, and citric acid creates a natural oral rehydration solution. The organic acids (citric, malic) in raw mango stimulate digestion and prevent the nausea common during extreme heat. Black salt adds iron and sulfur compounds. This drink is so deeply embedded in Indian heat-survival culture that it has been recommended in official NDMA heatwave guidelines. Each region has its own variant — Maharashtra boils the mango, Bihar and UP roast it on open flame for a smoky flavor.

properties.ayurvedic_virya: Sheeta (cooling) — unique because ripe mango is Ushna (heating)
properties.active_compounds: Ascorbic acid (Vitamin C 45mg), Citric acid, Malic acid, Pectin

ingredients.notes: Must be unripe/green — ripe mangoes are heating
Jaggery adds iron; adjust to taste
Essential — do not substitute with table salt

steps: Boil or pressure cook raw mangoes until soft (about 15 min). OR roast on direct flame until skin chars (traditional method).
Cool completely. Peel and extract all the pulp.
Blend pulp with sugar, black salt, roasted cumin, and mint until smooth.
Add cold water and mix well. Adjust sweetness and salt.
Strain if desired (optional). Serve chilled with ice and mint garnish.
preparation.storage_tip: Aam panna concentrate (without water) can be stored in the fridge for 5-7 days. Add water when serving. The concentrate can also be frozen in ice cube trays for up to a month.

variations.Maharashtra (Panha): Boiled mango pulp with jaggery, saffron, and cardamom
variations.Uttar Pradesh: Mango roasted on open flame (bharta style) for smoky flavor
variations.Gujarat (Baflo): With chaat masala and extra black salt

data_sources.name: IFCT 2017 — NIN, ICMR Hyderabad
data_sources.reference: Entry E042, Mango raw (Mangifera indica)

data_sources.name: NDMA Heatwave Guidelines 2024
data_sources.reference: Aam panna specifically recommended for heatstroke prevention

drinks.name: Sattu Sharbat
drinks.image_alt: Glass of sattu drink with lemon and mint
drinks.regional_origin: Bihar / Uttar Pradesh / Jharkhand / Madhya Pradesh

nutrition_per_serving_300ml.source: Calculated from IFCT 2017 (B011 roasted Bengal gram) + recipe proportions (2 tbsp sattu ~20g)

drinks.why_recommended_in_summer: Sattu is India's original protein shake — a roasted chickpea flour that has sustained farmers and laborers in the Gangetic plains through the most brutal summers for centuries. With 5.2g protein per glass, it provides sustained energy without the metabolic heat produced by digesting meat or eggs. The roasting process makes the proteins highly bioavailable and easy to digest. Sattu has a low glycemic index, preventing energy crashes in heat. The drink provides both hydration (water base) and nutrition (protein, fiber, iron) — making it the ideal replacement for a heavy meal when appetite drops in extreme heat. At roughly ₹5-7 per glass, it's one of the most cost-effective protein sources available. Traditional knowledge systems of Bihar classify sattu as 'thanda' (cold in nature), and modern analysis confirms its relatively low thermic effect compared to other protein sources.

ingredients.notes: Buy organic sattu or chane ka sattu from local store

steps: Add sattu flour to cold water in a glass or jug.
Whisk or stir vigorously to break up any lumps — this is important.
Add lemon juice, black salt, and roasted cumin powder.
Mix well again. The sattu will settle, so stir before every sip.
Garnish with mint leaves and optional green chilli.
Serve immediately — do not store.
preparation.storage_tip: Dry sattu flour lasts months in an airtight container. Once mixed with water, drink immediately — it settles and can ferment in heat.

variations.Sweet sattu (Meetha): Replace salt and cumin with jaggery or sugar — popular as a dessert drink
variations.Savory sattu (Namkeen): The recipe above — the more common version
variations.Sattu paratha filling: Mix sattu with onion, chilli, and spices — stuff in dough for protein-rich flatbread

data_sources.reference: Entry B011, Bengal gram roasted (Cicer arietinum) — base for sattu flour

drinks.name: ORS (Oral Rehydration Solution)
drinks.image_alt: ORS packet being dissolved in a glass of water

nutrition_per_serving_200ml.source: WHO ORS formulation standard (2006 low-osmolarity formula)

drinks.why_recommended_in_summer: ORS is the gold standard for treating dehydration from any cause, including heat-related illness. The WHO-formulated low-osmolarity ORS uses a specific ratio of glucose to sodium that activates the sodium-glucose co-transport mechanism in the small intestine, pulling water into the bloodstream far more efficiently than plain water. During a heatwave, when the body loses 1-2 liters of sweat per hour in extreme conditions, ORS can mean the difference between recovery and hospitalization. The NDMA specifically recommends keeping ORS packets at home during declared heatwaves. Available at every Indian pharmacy for ₹5-15 per packet, ORS is the single most important item in a heat emergency kit. It should be the FIRST thing given to anyone showing signs of heat exhaustion.

ingredients.notes: Available at any pharmacy without prescription. Brands: Electral, Enerzal, WHO ORS
Must be clean/boiled/filtered — do NOT use unclean water

steps: Wash hands before preparation.
Dissolve the entire ORS packet in 1 liter of clean drinking water.
Stir until fully dissolved. Do NOT add sugar, salt, or any other ingredient.
Sip small amounts frequently — do NOT gulp large quantities at once.
Discard unused solution after 24 hours.
preparation.storage_tip: Dry packets last years in a cool place. Once dissolved, use within 24 hours. Keep prepared solution covered and away from direct sunlight.
preparation.emergency_note: If ORS packets are unavailable, make at home: 6 teaspoons sugar + 1/2 teaspoon salt in 1 liter clean water. This is a temporary substitute only — get proper ORS as soon as possible.

data_sources.name: World Health Organization
data_sources.reference: WHO/UNICEF ORS formulation guidelines, 2006 low-osmolarity formula

data_sources.name: NDMA Heatwave Guidelines 2024
data_sources.reference: ORS recommended as essential heat emergency supply

data_sources.name: The Lancet
data_sources.reference: Oral rehydration therapy: sodium-glucose co-transport mechanism and clinical efficacy

data_sources.name: Indian Academy of Pediatrics
data_sources.reference: ORS guidelines for heat-related dehydration in children

drinks.name: Jaljeera
drinks.image_alt: Glass of jaljeera drink with mint garnish
drinks.why_recommended_in_summer: Jaljeera is India's classic digestive summer cooler. The cumin aids digestion (which slows in heat), mint provides cooling sensation via menthol, tamarind adds tanginess that stimulates saliva, and black salt replenishes sodium lost in sweat. It is one of the most popular street beverages during Indian summers.

steps: Dry roast 2 tbsp cumin seeds, grind to powder.
Blend with 15-20 mint leaves, 1 tbsp tamarind paste, 1 tsp black salt, pinch of asafoetida.
Mix paste in 1L cold water. Add lime juice.
Serve chilled with ice and fresh mint.
preparation.storage_tip: Jaljeera powder mix can be stored for weeks. Add to water fresh each time.

data_sources.name: IFCT 2017 — NIN, ICMR
data_sources.reference: Cumin, mint, tamarind individual entries

data_sources.name: Traditional Ayurvedic practice
data_sources.reference: Digestive cooler classification

drinks.name: Kokum Sharbat
drinks.image_alt: Deep purple kokum sharbat in a glass
drinks.why_recommended_in_summer: Kokum is a certified Ayurvedic coolant native to India's western coast. The Garcinia indica fruit has powerful anti-inflammatory properties and prevents acidity and sun-induced nausea. The India Perspectives government portal specifically recommends kokum juice as an effective summer beverage. It also helps with prickly heat and skin rashes common in summer.

steps: Soak 8-10 dried kokum rinds in 1 cup warm water for 30 min.
Squeeze out the juice, discard rinds.
Mix with cold water, sugar/jaggery, roasted cumin powder, black salt.
Serve chilled. Can also use ready-made kokum syrup.
preparation.storage_tip: Dried kokum lasts months. Kokum syrup keeps for weeks refrigerated.
variations: Sol kadhi with coconut milk (Konkani special)

data_sources.name: Research on Garcinia indica
data_sources.reference: Journal of Food Science & Technology

data_sources.name: Ayurvedic Pharmacopoeia of India

drinks.name: Bael Sharbat
drinks.image_alt: Thick bael wood apple sharbat in a glass
drinks.why_recommended_in_summer: Bael sharbat is arguably the most medicinal traditional Indian summer drink. It treats heat-induced constipation (very common during heatwaves), acts as a natural detoxifier for blood and liver, and helps prevent heatstroke. The very high potassium content (380mg/serving) rapidly restores electrolytes. Ayurveda classifies it as one of the best remedies for Pitta imbalance.

steps: Crack open ripe bael fruit, scoop out pulp.
Soak pulp in water for 1 hour, mash well.
Strain through a sieve, pressing to extract maximum juice.
Add jaggery, pinch of black pepper and cardamom. Serve chilled.
preparation.storage_tip: Prepare fresh — bael sharbat doesn't keep well. Consume within a few hours.
variations: Add a pinch of black salt for extra electrolytes

data_sources.name: IFCT 2017 — NIN, ICMR
data_sources.reference: Bael fruit (Aegle marmelos)

data_sources.name: India Perspectives, Govt of India
data_sources.reference: Bael can cure heatstroke and prevent dehydration effects

data_sources.name: Bhavaprakasha Nighantu
data_sources.reference: Bilva — cooling and digestive classification

drinks.name: Thandai
drinks.image_alt: Creamy thandai drink garnished with nuts in a glass
drinks.why_recommended_in_summer: Thandai literally means 'that which cools'. This rich North Indian drink combines milk with almonds, fennel seeds, cardamom, rose petals, melon seeds, and saffron — each ingredient chosen for its cooling properties in Ayurveda. While festive, it's traditionally consumed throughout summer in Rajasthan and UP where temperatures cross 45°C. Provides sustained energy and nutrition during heat when appetite is low.

steps: Soak overnight: 20 almonds, 1 tbsp melon seeds, 1 tbsp poppy seeds, 10 peppercorns.
Grind soaked ingredients with 1 tsp fennel seeds, 4-5 cardamom pods, few rose petals.
Mix paste into 500ml chilled milk. Add sugar to taste and saffron strands.
Strain if desired. Serve chilled with crushed ice and garnish with rose petals.
preparation.storage_tip: Thandai paste can be stored for a week. Mix into milk fresh each time.
variations: Add khus (vetiver) syrup for extra cooling

data_sources.name: Estimated from IFCT 2017 ingredients
data_sources.reference: Almonds, milk, fennel, cardamom

data_sources.name: Traditional Rajasthani practice
data_sources.reference: Summer cooling drink — thandai = coolness

drinks.name: Khus Sharbat
drinks.image_alt: Green khus vetiver sharbat in a tall glass
drinks.why_recommended_in_summer: Khus (vetiver) is a cooling herb known for controlling excessive thirst — a key problem during heatwaves. The aromatic grass contains iron, antioxidants, and minerals. Its distinctive green syrup is mixed with cold water or milk for an instantly refreshing drink. Vetiver roots are also used in traditional coolers (khus curtains) to cool rooms — the same cooling principle applies when consumed as a drink.

steps: Add 2-3 tbsp khus syrup to a glass of cold water or milk.
Stir well and add ice.
Optionally add sabja (basil) seeds for extra cooling.
Ready-made khus syrup is widely available (Rooh Afza, Mapro brands).
preparation.storage_tip: Khus syrup bottles last months unopened. Refrigerate after opening.

data_sources.name: Estimated from commercial khus syrup
data_sources.reference: Vetiveria zizanioides preparation

data_sources.name: Ayurvedic Pharmacopoeia
data_sources.reference: Ushira (vetiver) — cooling and thirst-quenching herb

drinks.name: Sugarcane Juice
drinks.image_alt: Fresh sugarcane juice in a glass with lime
drinks.why_recommended_in_summer: Sugarcane juice is nature's energy drink with extremely high potassium (800mg per glass) — essential for preventing dehydration and heatstroke. It provides instant energy from natural sugars without the harmful effects of processed soft drinks. Available on virtually every Indian street corner in summer. The addition of lime and ginger enhances both cooling and digestive properties.

steps: Buy from a clean street vendor with a mechanical press.
Ask for fresh lime and ginger to be added.
Drink immediately on the spot.
NEVER take home or store — it ferments within 15-20 minutes.
preparation.storage_tip: DO NOT STORE. Sugarcane juice oxidizes and ferments rapidly. Always consume fresh.

data_sources.name: IFCT 2017 — NIN, ICMR
data_sources.reference: Sugarcane juice (Saccharum officinarum)

data_sources.name: Traditional practice
data_sources.reference: Pan-India street beverage, electrolyte restoration

foods_to_avoid.name: Spicy Foods (excess chilli, pepper)
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Capsaicin in chillies activates TRPV1 heat receptors, triggering a thermoregulatory response that includes sweating and increased peripheral blood flow. While this provides temporary cooling through evaporation, it accelerates fluid loss and can worsen dehydration in already hot conditions. Spicy foods also increase metabolic heat generation and can cause acidity, heartburn, and GI distress — symptoms that compound the discomfort of heat exposure. Ayurveda classifies most pungent spices as 'Ushna virya' (heating potency) that directly aggravate Pitta dosha in summer.
alternatives: Use cooling spices instead: cumin, coriander, fennel, cardamom
Reduce chilli quantity by half or more in regular recipes
Add curd or raita as a cooling counterbalance when eating spiced food

data_sources.name: European Journal of Pharmacology
data_sources.reference: Capsaicin activation of TRPV1 receptors and thermoregulatory response

data_sources.name: Tata 1mg / Apollo Diagnostics
data_sources.reference: Foods to avoid during heatwave — spicy foods increase body temperature

data_sources.name: Charaka Samhita
data_sources.reference: Katu rasa (pungent taste) aggravates Pitta — contraindicated in Grishma Ritu

foods_to_avoid.name: Fried Foods (pakora, samosa, chips)
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Deep-fried foods are the worst dietary choice during extreme heat for three reasons: (1) Fat is the slowest macronutrient to digest, keeping the digestive system working hard for hours — this process generates significant metabolic heat (thermic effect of fat is 0-3% but the prolonged digestion time compounds it). (2) Oil-heavy foods cause sluggishness and reduced alertness, impairing the body's ability to recognize and respond to heat stress symptoms. (3) Excessive oil consumption in summer clogs skin pores, combining with sweat to cause acne, boils, and heat rash. Healthkart and NDMA guidelines both explicitly recommend avoiding fried foods during heatwaves.
alternatives: Roasted makhana (fox nuts) — light, cooling, and protein-rich
Baked snacks instead of fried
Fresh fruit chaat with chaat masala
Steamed sprouts with lemon

data_sources.name: Healthkart / Apollo Diagnostics
data_sources.reference: Foods to avoid in summer — fried foods hamper digestion and raise body temperature

data_sources.name: NDMA Heatwave Guidelines
data_sources.reference: Avoid heavy, oily foods during heatwave

data_sources.name: British Journal of Nutrition
data_sources.reference: Thermic effect of food — dietary fat and metabolic heat generation

foods_to_avoid.name: Alcohol
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Alcohol is a potent diuretic — it suppresses antidiuretic hormone (ADH/vasopressin), causing the kidneys to excrete more water than is consumed. A single beer can result in a net fluid loss of 350ml. In extreme heat, where the body is already losing 1-2 liters of sweat per hour, alcohol consumption can rapidly accelerate dehydration to dangerous levels. Alcohol also impairs thermoregulation by causing vasodilation (blood vessel expansion), which can paradoxically increase core body temperature. It impairs judgment, reducing the person's ability to recognize symptoms of heat exhaustion. Multiple studies confirm that alcohol consumption during heatwaves significantly increases the risk of heat-related death. This is the single most dangerous dietary choice during extreme heat.
alternatives: Aam panna — tangy and satisfying like a cocktail
Kokum sherbet — complex flavor profile
Jaljeera — spiced and appetizing
Sattu sharbat — filling and energizing

data_sources.name: Journal of Clinical Investigation
data_sources.reference: Alcohol suppression of ADH and diuretic effects — mechanism of dehydration

data_sources.name: Tata 1mg Health
data_sources.reference: Alcohol explicitly listed as food to avoid during heatwave

data_sources.name: International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health
data_sources.reference: Alcohol consumption as risk factor for heat-related mortality during heatwaves

data_sources.name: NDMA / WHO
data_sources.reference: Both organizations recommend avoiding alcohol during heat emergencies

foods_to_avoid.name: Tea and Coffee (excess)
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Caffeine is a methylxanthine that acts as a mild diuretic at doses above 300mg (roughly 3+ cups of chai). While moderate caffeine consumption (1-2 cups) may not cause significant net fluid loss, the hot temperature of chai and coffee raises core body temperature at a time when the body is struggling to cool down. Caffeine also stimulates the central nervous system, increasing heart rate and metabolic activity — both of which generate additional body heat. The tannic acid in tea can cause acidity, which worsens in summer. In Ayurveda, both tea and coffee are classified as 'Ushna' (heating) substances that aggravate Pitta.
alternatives: Buttermilk (chaas) — the Indian replacement for afternoon chai
Coconut water
Mint tea (herbal, zero caffeine) — provides ritual without the heat
Nimbu pani

data_sources.name: Journal of Science in Food and Agriculture
data_sources.reference: Caffeine as diuretic: dose-dependent effects on fluid balance

data_sources.name: Zee News / Apollo Diagnostics
data_sources.reference: Caffeine increases body heat — reduce intake during summer

data_sources.name: Modern Ayurvedic practitioners
data_sources.reference: Tea and coffee classified as Pitta-aggravating beverages

foods_to_avoid.name: Stale / Leftover Food
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: This is a food SAFETY issue, not just nutrition. Bacteria like Salmonella, E. coli, and Staphylococcus aureus double every 20 minutes in the 'danger zone' (4°C–60°C). In Indian summer, ambient temperatures of 40-47°C create the perfect incubator. Food left at room temperature for more than 2 hours in summer is potentially hazardous. Cooked rice is especially dangerous — Bacillus cereus spores survive cooking and germinate rapidly in warm conditions, producing toxins that cause vomiting and diarrhea. Food poisoning during heatwaves is particularly dangerous because the resulting diarrhea and vomiting cause severe dehydration on top of heat-related fluid loss, creating a compounding emergency. Indian summers see a documented 2-3x spike in foodborne illness cases.
alternatives: Cook fresh meals in smaller batches — only what you'll eat in one sitting
Refrigerate cooked food within 30 minutes — do not let it cool at room temperature
When in doubt, throw it out — the risk is not worth it
Prefer no-cook meals: curd rice, salads, fruits

data_sources.name: FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India)
data_sources.reference: Food safety guidelines during summer — danger zone temperatures and bacterial growth

data_sources.name: Indian Journal of Gastroenterology
data_sources.reference: Seasonal spike in food-borne illness during Indian summer months — epidemiological data

data_sources.name: WHO Food Safety

foods_to_avoid.name: Ice Cream & Ice-Cold Water
foods_to_avoid.image_alt: Ice cream cone melting in heat
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Ice cream and ice-cold water provide temporary relief but are counterproductive. Your body expends energy warming the cold food to body temperature — this actually generates metabolic heat. Ice-cold water can shock the digestive system, causing cramps and slowing digestion. Ayurveda and modern nutrition both recommend cool (not ice-cold) beverages for effective cooling. Ice cream also has high sugar and fat which increase metabolic heat production.
alternatives: Cool (not frozen) buttermilk
Room temperature water from matka (earthen pot)
Chilled fruit instead of ice cream

data_sources.name: Ayurvedic principle
data_sources.reference: Ice-cold food aggravates Agni (digestive fire)

data_sources.name: AIIMS Nutrition Department
data_sources.reference: Cool vs cold water absorption rates

foods_to_avoid.name: Carbonated Soft Drinks
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: NDMA specifically warns against carbonated soft drinks during heatwaves. They are diuretics that cause net water loss despite feeling refreshing. The high sugar (35-40g per can) causes an energy spike followed by a crash, worsening heat fatigue. They provide zero electrolytes while creating a false sense of hydration. Phosphoric acid in colas can worsen dehydration by increasing urination.
alternatives: Nimbu pani with black salt
Jaljeera
Coconut water
Plain soda with lime (if craving fizz)

data_sources.name: NDMA Heat Wave Do's and Don'ts
data_sources.reference: Avoid carbonated soft drinks which dehydrate the body

data_sources.name: ICMR Dietary Guidelines 2024
data_sources.reference: Limit consumption of sugary beverages

foods_to_avoid.name: Excess Red Meat & Eggs
foods_to_avoid.image_alt: Heavy meat dish on a plate
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: NDMA guidelines warn against high-protein food during heat waves. Protein has the highest thermic effect of food (TEF) — your body generates 20-30% more heat digesting protein compared to 5-10% for carbs. Red meat takes 4-6 hours to digest, keeping metabolic heat elevated throughout peak heat hours. During extreme heat, this extra internal heat load can push the body toward heat exhaustion.
alternatives: Light dal (moong dal)
Curd rice
Paneer in moderate quantity
Sprouts salad

data_sources.name: NDMA Heat Wave Do's and Don'ts
data_sources.reference: Avoid high-protein food during heatwaves

data_sources.name: Thermic effect of food
data_sources.reference: Protein TEF 20-30% vs Carbohydrate TEF 5-10%

foods_to_avoid.name: Leftover / Reheated Food
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: NDMA explicitly warns against eating stale food during heatwaves. Bacteria multiply 2x faster for every 10°C rise above 20°C — at 40°C ambient temperature, food left out for 2 hours can have dangerous bacterial counts. Food poisoning combined with dehydration during a heatwave can be fatal. Even refrigerated leftovers should be reheated thoroughly. Cooked food should be consumed within 4-6 hours of preparation as per FSSAI guidelines.
alternatives: Cook smaller portions — eat fresh
No-cook meals: fruit bowls, curd rice, salads
If storing, refrigerate immediately and reheat to boiling

meal_plans.source: Compiled from NDMA guidelines + Ayurvedic Ritucharya (seasonal regimen) + nutritionist recommendations from AIIMS, Apollo, and Amrita Hospital

lunch: Curd rice with pickle and papad
Curd rice ONLY — skip heavy curries entirely
Skip meal if not hungry — hydrate with coconut water instead
snack: Nimbu pani with extra black salt
dinner: Fruit meal if appetite is low — do not force heavy food
